query: Velo 3.0 complaints OR frustrating OR broken

results.title: Fuji Newest 3.0 Road Bike Review | DC Rainmaker
results.content:
March 18, 2010 at 7:02 am #22

    Hey DC,

    Thanks for the review, I actually just bought a new ’09 3.0 and I love it. I just started getting into cycling and I’m doing my first tri this summer. Totally right about the shifting, a little rough at first. Hopefully I can get it tweaked so it’s better or maybe upgrade the derailuers, but it does what it needs to do for now. If anyone is looking to get into the sport and is looking at a bike that is solid, no frills, and won’t break the bank, I’d say this bike fits the bill. My only complaint is the stem… it is adjustable which is nice, but I hit a speed bump and it came loose, no injuries just scared the crap out of me. Either tighten it up really good or upgrade to a solid one piece stem. Otherwise I love it.

    -B [...] It seems like most of you have been buying a lot of the marketing hype that says full carbon skinny tired race light bikes are better, because, well, they’re full carbon, skinny tired, and race light. I commute 33 miles a day, so I bought my Newest 3.0 for other reasons, like the ability to get the bars even with superbly comfortable velo saddle, and clearance to run slightly wider 28mm wide tires with fenders. As for the crappy shifting, I had no issue withthe stock shifters, because I bought my bike at a shop known for top notch mechanics (Performance bike is known for having “ok” machanics). The shifting is even beter now that a put on ultegra bar end shifters (friction mode). Try tightening the cable tension using the barrel adjusters on the downtube bosses, a lot of bike shop [...] with others on the team, many on much better bikes, she had some breakdowns, such as 1 broken spoke, broken left shifter, several needed adjustments to deraillers, etc., but the bike did fine. The main things done to it before the trip were a better chain, better brakes, replacing the saddle twice to find one that might work, but the best money i spent was on an extremely precise fit at gran fondo bike shop. I told my daughter i would treat her to a new bike when she finished the tour, at least a nice orbea or bianchi, but she is so attached to her bike, she’s not interested. ( i forgot, we also replaced the tires with some great continentals. the bike did great, especially considering days were as long as 141 miles. I realize that by the time we made all the upgrade we could have bought

results.title: Velowave Ranger 3.0 Review – Is This the Best Value Adventure Ebike?
results.content: That's not the highest I've seen on a 750 W motor fat tire bike, but that should be plenty of power to get us up the big hill whenever we go test it over there. So far, I'm really pleased with the comfort level on this bike. The seat is comfortable. These ergonomic grips are fantastic. The twist shifting is really nice. It's got a mountain bike style setup here. So, it's really comfortable for me since I come from a mountain biking background. But overall, the bike feels like it's in really good control. I don't feel unstable on it. And that's a big benefit of having fat tires is that they just make the whole ride super stable. And they give you an added like cushion whenever you're going over some bumpy stuff. But we do have a nice suspension up here. This one is really eating up a lot [...] very slow uh buildup, so it's not jolty, which is really nice cuz sometimes you switch pedal assist levels on some bikes and it gets very very jolty. This one was pretty smooth. Looking at about 12 mph on this pedal assist, pedal assist 3 should get some more power. I feel it kick in there a little bit more aggressively than pedal assist 2. And we are going. We're still climbing, but it looks like we're going to max out about 17.2 m an hour on pedal assist 3, pedal assist 4. There it is. Kicked in. Definitely getting up to speed here. And we are 22 mph on pedal assist 4. Now pedal assist 5, they claim that this will go 28 mph. We'll see what it gets up to. Right now we're about 25 12 26 26 and 1/2 uh 27. It may do it. It might hit 28. There it is. 28.2 28.2 miles an hour. So, it did hit [...] fast. What's really nice about that is if you're at a stoplight and you want to accelerate with your throttle and then start pedaling after you get through the intersection, you can do that. Now, let's do the reverse. I'm pedaling. Let's go ahead and switch over to throttle. Instant. They've got this down. That was really, really good. Very impressed with that. That's one test that not a lot of people do, but in real riding, that is a very important test that you want that to be responsive. You don't want delays on any of that whenever you're switching between whether you're going through throttle to pedal assist or pedal assist to throttle. You don't want a delay. Okay, let's head over and do the 0 to 20 mph test. This is where we're going to test the throttle and the pedal assist and

results.title: VELO Nicotine Pouches: A Brutally Honest Review (Updated 2025) - Snus Boys
results.content:
i cant believe im the only review stating this. EVERY 2 out of 3 cans of velo ive purchased in the past ALWAYS has at least one if not multiple of the pouches that has leaked all the powder out which then gets all over the other pouches rendering them useless w/o a lot of cleaning. i contacted velo multiple times about this telling them that the ends of the pouches are not sealed well and leak of which they have done nothing about. at first velo seemed to have the edge over ZYN b/ velo has 20 pouches vs ZYN having 15. once you consider all the leaking velo pouchs i switched to ZYN which i found to have slightly larger pouches and MUCH better LONGER lasting taste. i began using these wintergreen pouches to help me kick my yrs long dip habit. i highly recommend any velo users to switch [...] Reply

results.title: VELO Nicotine Pouches Review: One of the Best? – The Snus King
results.content:
Overall the pouch composition of VELO is suited to target audience. The pouches are light, fluffy, and always fresh. You would expect nothing less from a corporate giant like BAT in producing a perfectly balanced pouch. They are comfortable, and consistent, which is more than can be said for some independent brands. Some pouches are kept in poor heated conditions which leads to a drying of ingredients, causing lumps in the pouch. Or, the pouches split in the can (and sometimes in your mouth). I have not experienced anything of the sort with VELO pouches.

## VELO Strength & Performance in Review [...] ### The Strength Seems Fitted for Purpose

It is fair to say that you can feel a buzz while using VELO. As someone accustomed to nicotine pouches it would be unfair to judge the 4mg - 6mg pouches. The nicotine level, for my own personal tolerance, would never be enough - which is why I tried my best to use them as early in the day as possible. My manufactured science of dehydration and sleep was still not enough to feel the effects I would have hoped for. That being said, as someone that has consistently reviewed 100mg plus pouches, 4mg was always punching above its weight. [...] There was only one fair way of me quantifying the effects of the lower level dot strengths (if you need to know more about dot strengths check out our VELO Dot System article). I had to use the lower level strength pouches at the first thing in the morning, while dehydrated and half asleep. In doing so, I was able to quantify the effects against what I would expect in pouches at that level. For me, the entry level pouches between 4mg - 8mg, were more mild than I would have expected. At 4mg, I felt literally nothing, and the same was true on some mornings at 8mg.

At 10mg, I could feel some effects, but still, the effects were a lot weaker than I would have expected from a 10mg pouch.

## Final Form Through Function [...] Although the current state of global affairs makes it slightly more expensive for non-UK customers to obtain a frameset—my estimates sit just over $2,000 with shipping at the time of writing—it’s still more than worth it for what has to be one of the most well-thought-out frames on the market.

Bikepacking Bikes [...] Fairlight Secan 3.0 Review

While the gravel category continues to see innovations that stretch and tear the fabric of what we understand the term to mean, what Fairlight has landed on with the Secan 3.0 is near perfection. There isn’t a spot wrong or a molecule out of place. Seriously. If the task is to create a steel gravel bike of this form, this is as close as we get to the Platonic ideal. And that’s a weird thing to say about a class of bike that I feel has always been endearingly flawed. I don’t think frames following this silhouette were ever meant to sport such wide tires and, as such, have become a fun, sub-optimal way to see how far your skill can take you on rough terrain.
